2032017558140834256.text:
Karpathy大神的autoresearch思路超简单：把一个单GPU小型LLM训练框架（nanochat版）交给AI代理，让它自主编辑https://t.co/lBSDdGgbHa代码、固定跑5分钟训练、算val_bpb（越低越好），只保留进步的版本，循环迭代。

人类只改program.md写高层次目标和提示，AI干所有低级实验优化，一晚上能跑几百次实验，早上醒来就有日志和更好模型。

核心就是自动化ML研究循环！Repo在这里：https://t.co/dWOXRic6JA

source_queries: (fineweb OR val_bpb) ("bits per byte" OR bpb OR quantization OR tokenizer OR recurrence)
2032106386390175771.text:
Great question. Experiment goals are really important. In the autoresearch project, every experiment reports val_bpb (validation bits per byte), which is an objective output metric. 

Each agent claims a hypothesis, runs it in isolation, and publishes the full result, including source code, under its own namespace in shared memory. If two agents try conflicting changes, that's completely fine, both results get recorded. The result with the lower bpb becomes the new global best. 

Agents read the current best config before each run, but only write to their own space, so there's no conflict resolution needed at the write level.

source_queries: (fineweb OR val_bpb) ("bits per byte" OR bpb OR quantization OR tokenizer OR recurrence)
2032643309684830248.text:
This just got pushed on the site as click-to-copy agent instructions:

# autolab contributor

You are an autonomous ML research contributor for autolab, a distributed project to push down val_bpb (validation bits per byte) on a small GPT language model.

autolab coordinates a community of contributors. You submit patches (diffs to https://t.co/Eip8ZCNWy9). Each patch goes through automated security review, then GPU workers run the experiment. If your patch achieves a new best val_bpb, it becomes the master — the starting point for all future experiments. You earn reputation for each improvement.

2034315602618175846.text:
OpenAI is about to launch "Parameter Golf" challenge, $1M in compute grants

> train the best language model that fits in a 16MB artifact and trains in under 10 minutes on 8xH100s, evaluated by compression on the FineWeb validation set (tokenizer-agnostic, bits per byte).

https://t.co/4cst5ToEM8

inspired by @karpathy 's NanoGPT speedrunning

The challenge runs from March 18th to April 30th.

In June, they plan to hire a small cohort of early-career researchers, targeting current undergraduate students and recent graduates, including Olympiad medalists and elite competitors.

2034642300333412562.text:
Day 1 on OpenAI's Parameter Golf challenge. Build the best language model that fits in 16MB, scored by compression on FineWeb. Ran 30 experiments overnight on my MacBook Air. Here's what I found.

Core idea: depth recurrence. Instead of 9 unique transformer layers, use 3 blocks and loop them 3x. Same depth, 1/3 the parameters. Use the freed budget for wider layers. First test beat the baseline
  immediately.

Iterated fast by evaluating on 10% of the validation set. Brought each experiement from 30 min down to 8 min.

Things that didn't work:
  • Smear gate + backout from the NanoGPT speedrun, made things worse
  • 2 unique layers, not enough diversity
  • More than 3 recurrences, diminishing returns

The pivot: wider models looked worse on 1 data shard. Thought something was broken. Turned out they were just data-starved. Scaled to 10 shards and everthing flipped.

  d1024 → 1.996 BPB
  d896 → 2.010
  d768 → 2.050

A 5000 step training got down to 1.589 BPB on a MacBook. Leaderboard baseline is 1.224 on 8xH100s. Gap should close with more data and compute.

Built an adaptive sweep system that analyzes past results and picks the next config to try. Ran 30 experiements overnight.

Next steps: Winning model compresses to 20MB so need to get it under 16MB. Trying QAT and finding the right size under budget. Then hopefully getting some H100 time.

  #ParameterGolf
